{{Short description|Friction, disagreement, or discord between people}}
{{Conflict resolution sidebar}}
A '''conflict''' is a [[scenario|situation]] in which [[Acceptance|unacceptable]] differences in interests, [[expectation (epistemic)|expectations]], [[values]], or [[opinion]]s occur between [[individual]]s, or between or in [[Social group|groups]].

==Definitions==
Depending on the source, there are different definitions for conflicts:

* Disagreements, discrepancies, and frictions that occur when the actions or beliefs of one or more members of the group are unacceptable to one or more other group members and are rejected by them.<ref name="Forsyth2019" />
* An interaction between actors (individuals, groups, organizations, etc.), where at least one actor experiences incompatibilities in thinking/imagination/perception and/or feeling and/or wanting with the other actor (the other actors) in such a way that in realizing an impairment by another actor (the other actors) occurs.<ref name="glasl">{{Cite book|title=Conflict Management and Industrial Relations|first=Friedrich|last=Glasl|editor-first1=Gerard B. J.|editor-last1=Bomers|editor-first2=Richard B.|editor-last2=Peterson|date=July 15, 1982|publisher=Springer Netherlands|pages=119–140|via=Springer Link|doi=10.1007/978-94-017-1132-6_6}}</ref>
* Contradictory interests that are represented by different people or groups of people and who are dependent on each other in achieving their interests (or at least believe this).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Schwarz |first=Gerhard |url=https://link.springer.com/10.1007/978-3-8349-4598-3 |title=Konfliktmanagement: Konflikte erkennen, analysieren, lösen |date=2014 |publisher=Gabler Verlag |isbn=978-3-8349-4597-6 |publication-place=Wiesbaden |page=36 |language=de |doi=10.1007/978-3-8349-4598-3}}</ref>
* Interactive processes that manifest themselves in incompatibility, disagreement, or dissonance within or between social entities.<ref>{{citation |author=M. Afzalur Rahim |date=2011 |isbn=978-1-4128-1456-0 |language=en |publisher=Transaction Publishers |title=Managing Conflict in Organizations}}<!-- auto-translated from German by Module:CS1 translator --></ref>
* A state of tension that arises because there are irreconcilable contradictions between two or more parties with regard to a certain good.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pfetsch |first=Frank R. |title=Konflikt und Konfliktbewältigung. Beispiele für Formen zwischenstaatlicher Auseinandersetzungen |date=January 1994 |publisher=Klett-Schulbuchverl |isbn=3-12-490400-1 |publication-place=Stuttgart |language=de |author-link=Frank R. Pfetsch}}</ref>
* Activities that take place when conscious beings (individuals or groups) want to take actions that do not match their desires, needs, or obligations.<ref>{{cite book |first1=Michael |last1=Nicholson |title=Rationality and the analysis of international conflict |publisher=Cambridge University Press |date=1992 |isbn=0-521-39125-3 |language=de |oclc=23687612}}</ref>
* Situations where hostile behavior occurs.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Nye |first=Robert D. |title=Conflict Among Humans: Some Basic Psychological and Social-psychological Considerations |date=1973 |publisher=Springer Publishing Company |isbn=978-0-8261-1371-9}}</ref>
* Behavior that deliberately hinders the achievement of another's goals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schmidt |first1=Stuart M. |last2=Kochan |first2=Thomas A. |date=1972 |title=Conflict: Toward Conceptual Clarity |journal=Administrative Science Quarterly |volume=17 |issue=3 |page=359 |doi=10.2307/2392149 |jstor=2392149}}</ref>
* State of objective incompatibility between values or goals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bernard |first=J. |date=1951-03-01 |title=The Conceptualization of Intergroup Relations: With Special Reference to Conflict |url=https://academic.oup.com/sf/article-lookup/doi/10.2307/2572412 |journal=Social Forces |volume=29 |issue=3 |pages=243–251 |doi=10.2307/2572412 |jstor=2572412 |issn=0037-7732 |author-link=Jessie Bernard|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Classification==
[[File:Conflict (1936) 1.jpg|thumb|[[Jean Rogers]], [[John Wayne]], and [[Ward Bond]] in the film ''[[Conflict (1936 film)|Conflict]]'' (1936)]]
In cases of [[intragroup conflict]], there is a conflict between the overall goals of the general group, and the goals of at least one person in that group.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Sidorenkov|first1=Andrey V|last2=Borokhovski|first2=Evgueni F|last3=Kovalenko|first3=Viktor A|date=October 2018|title=Group size and composition of work groups as precursors of intragroup conflicts|journal=Psychology Research and Behavior Management|volume= 11|pages=511–523|doi=10.2147/prbm.s178761|pmid=30498379|pmc=6207408|issn=1179-1578|doi-access=free}}</ref> The disagreements may also be examples of [[interpersonal conflict]], a conflict between two or more people. Intrapersonal conflicts are conflicts occurring in an individual, for example a bad [[conscience]] or an identity conflict. Intergroup conflict is conflict between two or more groups.

===Types===
More specific types of conflict include the following.

* Content conflict occurs when individuals disagree about how to deal with a certain issue. This can be a good thing as it has the potential to stimulate discussion and increase motivation.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Social psychology in sport|date=2007|publisher=Human Kinetics|last1=Jowett|first1=Sophia|last2=Lavallee|first2=David|isbn=978-0-7360-5780-6|location=Champaign, IL|oclc=64770988}}</ref>
* Relationship conflict occurs when individuals disagree about one another. This relational conflicts decreases performance, loyalty, satisfaction and commitment, and causes individuals to be irritable, negative and suspicious. This stems from interpersonal incompatibilities. It is an awareness of frictions caused by frustrations, annoyance, and irritations. Relationship conflict is comparable to affective and cognitive conflict as defined by Amason and Pinkley, respectively.<ref name="Jehn-2001">{{Cite journal|last1=Jehn|first1=K. A.|last2=Mannix|first2=E. A.|title=The Dynamic Nature of Conflict: A Longitudinal Study of Intragroup Conflict and Group Performance|date=2001-04-01|journal=Academy of Management Journal|language=en|volume=44|issue=2|pages=238–251|jstor=3069453|issn=0001-4273}}</ref>
* [[Process conflict]] refers to disagreement over the group's approach to the task, its methods, and its group process.<ref name="Jehn-2001" /> They note that although relationship conflict and process conflict are harmful, task conflict is found to be beneficial since it encourages diversity of opinions, although care should be taken so it does not develop into a process or relationship conflict.<ref name="Jehn-2001" />
* Task conflict is related to disagreements in viewpoints and opinion about a particular task in group settings. It is associated with two interrelated and beneficial effects. The first is group decision quality. Task conflict encourages greater [[Cognition|cognitive]] understanding of the issue being discussed. This leads to better decision making for the groups that use task conflict.<ref name="Jehn-2001" /> The second is [[affective]] acceptance of [[Group decision-making|group decisions]]. Task conflict can lead to increased satisfaction with the group decision and a desire to stay in the group.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Järvisalo|first1=J.|last2=Saris|first2=N. E.|date=1975-09-15|title=Action of propranolol on mitochondrial functions--effects on energized ion fluxes in the presence of valinomycin|journal=Biochemical Pharmacology|volume=24|issue=18|pages=1701–1705|doi=10.1016/0006-2952(75)90009-x|issn=0006-2952|pmid=13}}</ref>
* Affective conflict is an emotional conflict developed from interpersonal incompatibilities and disputes. It often produces suspicion, distrust, and hostility. Therefore, it is seen as a negative kind of conflict and an obstacle to those who experience it and is described as "dysfunctional."<ref name="Amason-1997">{{Cite journal|last1=Amason|first1=Allen C.|last2=Sapienza|first2=Harry J.|date=August 1997|title=The Effects of Top Management Team Size and interaction Norms on Cognitive and Affective Conflict|journal=Journal of Management|language=en|volume=23|issue=4|pages=495–516|doi=10.1177/014920639702300401|s2cid=143833786|issn=0149-2063}}</ref>
* Cognitive conflict occurs during tasks and comes from a difference in perspective and judgement. It improves decision making and allows for the freer exchange of information between group members. Cognitive conflict is seen as a positive tension that promotes good group work.<ref name="Amason-1997" />

===Geography===
Conflicts can also be categorized geographically, as in the [[Global North and Global South|North-South conflict]] and the [[Cold War|East-West conflict]]. Other examples are [[territorial conflict]]s such as the [[Kosovo War]], the [[Gulf War|Iraq-Iran War]], the [[Middle East conflict]], the [[China-Taiwan Relations|China-Taiwan conflict]] and the [[Korean Conflict]].

===Persons or Groups===
Likewise, conflicts can be categorised according to the people involved. Areas in which conflicts frequently occur are, for example, in the [[family]], between parents, between siblings or between parents and children, among friends and acquaintances, in [[social group|groups]], in [[school]], in [[nature]], in [[business]] between companies, employers or employees,<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Overton | first1=Amy | last2=Lowry | first2=Ann | title=Conflict Management: Difficult Conversations with Difficult People | journal=Clinics in Colon and Rectal Surgery | publisher=Georg Thieme Verlag KG | volume=26 | issue=4 | date=2013-11-20 | issn=1531-0043 | doi=10.1055/s-0033-1356728 | doi-access=free | pages=259–264 |pmid=24436688 |pmc=3835442}}</ref> in [[science]],<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pietschmann |first=Herbert |title=Würzburger medizinhistorische Mitteilungen |year=2005 |volume=24 |pages=425–433 |language=de |chapter=Vom Umgang mit wissenschaftlichen Konflikten. Movement on the waterfront}}</ref> between [[generation]]s ([[generational conflict]]), between [[ethnicity|ethnic groups]] (ethnic conflict) or within or between states (see [[peace research]]).

The following are examples of conflict that could be either intragroup or intergroup conflict.
* [[Conflict of interest]] is involvement in multiple interests which could possibly corrupt the motivation or decision-making.<ref>{{Citation |title=Conflict Of Interest |year=2005 |place=Thousand Oaks California |publisher=Sage Publications, Inc. |doi=10.4135/9781412950558.n97 |isbn=978-0-7619-2609-2 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Evaluation}}</ref>
* [[Cultural conflict]] is a type of conflict that occurs when different cultural values and beliefs clash.<ref name="Lee-2004">{{Cite book |last=Lee |first=Yueh-Ting |title=The psychology of ethnic and cultural conflict |publisher=Praeger |year=2004 |isbn=978-0-275-97983-6 |location=Westport, Conn}}</ref>
** Intellectual conflict is a subclass of [[cultural conflict]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=MacDonald |first=Kevin |date=2009-04-01 |title=Evolution, Psychology, and a Conflict Theory of Culture |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |language=en |volume=7 |issue=2 |article-number=147470490900700206 |doi=10.1177/147470490900700206 |issn=1474-7049 |s2cid=4247168 |doi-access=free}}</ref>
* [[Ethnic conflict]] is conflict between two or more contending ethnic groups.<ref name="Lee-2004" />
* [[Organizational conflict]] is discord caused by opposition of needs, values, and interests between people working together.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Organizational conflict {{!}} definition in the Cambridge English Dictionary |url=https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/organizational-conflict |access-date=2020-07-18 |website=dictionary.cambridge.org |language=en-US}}</ref>
* [[Role conflict]] involves incompatible demands placed upon a person in a manner that makes accomplishing both troublesome.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Definition of role conflict {{!}} Dictionary.com |url=https://www.dictionary.com/browse/role-conflict |access-date=2020-07-18 |website=www.dictionary.com |language=en}}</ref>
* [[Social conflict]] is the struggle for supremacy or autonomy between social classes.
* [[Work–family conflict]] involves incompatible demands between the work and family roles of an individual.<ref>{{Cite web |title=What Is Work-Family Conflict? {{!}} Baylor Online |url=https://onlinemasters.baylor.edu/articles/work-family-conflict.aspx |access-date=2020-07-18 |website=Baylor University MBA Online Degree Program |language=en}}</ref>

==Development==
[[File:Fighting after school in central Jamaica - all shoelaces are open (32361821164).jpg|thumb|School children fighting in Jamaica]]
Conflicts are not static events in themselves, but develop a dynamic that can become uncontrollable in extreme cases. The course of a conflict can be divided into four phases:<ref>{{Cite book |last=Berkel |first=Karl |title=Management-Lexikon |year=1985 |editor-last=Fritz Neske |volume=II |language=de |chapter=Konfliktmanagement |editor-last2=Markus Wiener}}</ref>

{| class="wikitable" style="padding:1em; vertical-align:top; border:2px;"
|-
! Phase of conflict
! Name
! Description
|-
| Phase I
| Latent phase || The conflict situation emerges
|-
| Phase II
| Conscience || Both parties recognise the conflict
|-
| Phase III
| Action || Both parties react based on their thoughts and emotions
|-
| Phase IV
| Interactions || Both parties interact with alternating conflict modes
|}

=== Escalation ===
Beyond that a conflict can further escalate. Models of escalation in conflicts are the [[Friedrich Glasl's model of conflict escalation]],<ref name="glasl"/> the [[conflict curve]] by [[Michael S. Lund]]<ref name="Bösch">{{Cite book |last=Bösch |first=Richard |url=https://oxfordre.com/internationalstudies/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780190846626.001.0001/acrefore-9780190846626-e-82 |title=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of International Studies |date=2017-11-20 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-084662-6 |chapter=Conflict Escalation |doi=10.1093/acrefore/9780190846626.013.82}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Saunders |first=H. H. |year=1998 |title=Review of Preventing Violent Conflicts: A Strategy for Preventive Diplomacy, by M. S. Lund; The Management of Conflict: Interpretations and Interests in Comparative Perspective, by M. H. Ross |journal=Political Psychology |volume=19 |issue=4 |pages=859–865 |jstor=3791880}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Lund |first=Michael S. |title=Preventing violent conflicts: A strategy for preventive diplomacy |date=1996 |publisher=US Institute of Peace Press |isbn=978-1-878379-52-8 |publication-place=Washington, D.C}}</ref> and the [[hourglass model]] by [[Oliver Ramsbotham]].<ref name="Bösch" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Ramsbotham |first1=Oliver |title=Contemporary Conflict Resolution |last2=Miall |first2=Hugh |last3=Woodhouse |first3=Tom |date=2011-04-11 |publisher=Polity |isbn=978-0-7456-4973-3}}</ref> When an escalation is initiated by one party there often is a sequence of escalation behaviour: [[Question|requests]], [[demand]]s, [[Anger|angry remarks]], [[threat]]s, [[harassment]] and [[abuse]].<ref name="Deutsch2006">{{cite book | veditors=((Deutsch, M.)) | date= 2006 | title=The handbook of conflict resolution: theory and practice | publisher=Jossey-Bass | edition=2nd | isbn=978-0-7879-8058-0}}</ref>

Models with a fixed order of stages of conflict escalation have been criticized for not representing the probabilistic nature of conflicts.<ref>{{Cite encyclopedia |title=Conflict Escalation |encyclopedia=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of International Studies |last=Bösch |first=Richard |date=2017 |doi=10.1093/acrefore/9780190846626.013.82 |isbn=978-0-19-084662-6}}</ref>

== Behavior ==
[[File:Graham's Hierarchy of Disagreement-en.svg|thumb|Hierarchy of arguments according to their escalation potential by Graham]] 
The various ways in which people react to conflict situations have been presented by [[Gerhard Schwarz]], sometimes in reference to Eric Lippmann, as follows:<ref>{{cite book |author=Jürg Kuster, Eugen Huber, Robert Lippmann, Alphons Schmid, Emil Schneider, Urs Witschi, Roger Wüst |title=Handbook of Project Management |publisher=Springer-Verlag |date=2008 |isbn=978-3-540-76432-8 |pages=258 = }}</ref><ref name="PENTA">{{cite book |author=Hans Fischer |title=Learning to Lead Sustainably: The Comprehensive Leadership Concept PENTA for Sustainable Leadership Impact |publisher=vdf Hochschulverlag AG |date=2015 |isbn=978-3-7281-3667-1|page=189}}</ref>

* [[Flight]] (Evasion, Avoidance)
* [[Fight]] (Enforcement, Destruction)
* [[Hierarchy|Subordination]] (Adaptation, Concession, Unilateral Acceptance)
* [[Delegation]] (of the problem to another instance)
* [[Compromise]] (Agreement with advantages and disadvantages on both sides)
* [[Consensus decision-making|Consensus]] (Cooperation)

These behavior patterns can lead to the solution or dissolution of a conflict in different situations. While the first-mentioned stages are anti- or confrontational in character, the last-mentioned stages represent forms of constructive conflict resolution - with consensus as the highest (to be learned) form.<ref name="PENTA" /><ref>{{cite book |author=Norbert J. Heigl |title=Understanding and Controlling Conflicts |publisher=Springer |date=2014 |isbn=978-3-658-04584-5 |page=24 }}</ref> [[Paul Graham (programmer)|Paul Graham]] divided forms of argument hierarchically according to their escalation potential and the quality of the argument.<ref>{{cite web |access-date=2022-03-09 |author=[[Paul Graham (programmer)|Paul Graham]] |date=2008 |title=How to Disagree |url=http://www.paulgraham.com/disagree.html}}</ref> In the ''dual concern model'', conflict types are divided along the two dimensions: orientation towards one's own goal or orientation towards the goal of the conflict partner.<ref name="Forsyth2019">{{cite book | vauthors=((Forsyth, D. R.)) | date= 2019 | title=Group dynamics | publisher=Cengage | edition=7th | isbn=978-1-337-40885-1}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors=((Wallensteen, P.)) | date= 2015 | title=Understanding conflict resolution | publisher=SAGE | edition=4th | isbn=978-1-4739-0210-7}}
</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dickmann |first=Christian R. |title=Konflikte lösen: Was Sie als Führungskraft über Konflikte im betrieblichen Umfeld wissen sollten |publisher=epubli |year=2015 |isbn=978-3-7375-2373-8 |page=22 |language=de |trans-title=Resolving Conflicts: What You Should Know as a Leader about Conflicts in the Corporate Environment}}</ref> [[Personality test]]s for conflict behavior are the Kraybill Conflict Style Inventory,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kraybill |first=Ronald |title=Style Matters: The Kraybill Conflict Style Inventory |publisher=Riverhouse |year=2005 |isbn=0-9755071-2-5}}</ref> the [[open-source license]]d "Ethics Position Questionnaire"<ref>{{cite journal | last1=O'Boyle | first1=Ernest H. | last2=Forsyth | first2=Donelson R. |author-link2=Donelson R. Forsyth | title=Individual differences in ethics positions: The EPQ-5 | journal=PLOS ONE | publisher=Public Library of Science (PLoS) | volume=16 | issue=6 | date=2021-06-21 | issn=1932-6203 | doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0251989 | doi-access=free | article-number=e0251989 |pmc=8216522  |pmid= 34153037 | bibcode=2021PLoSO..1651989O }}</ref> and the [[Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument]]. Further, more general and comprehensive personality tests are [[Hogan Assessments|Leadership Derailers]],<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Hogan |first1=Robert |title=The Hogan Guide |last2=Hogan |first2=Joyce |last3=Warrenfeltz |first3=Rodney |date=2007 |publisher=Hogan Assessment Systems |isbn=978-0-9816457-6-6 |language=en}}</ref> [[Social value orientation]],<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Murphy | first1=Ryan O. | last2=Ackermann | first2=Kurt A. | last3=Handgraaf | first3=Michel J. J. | title=Measuring Social Value Orientation | journal=Judgment and Decision Making | volume=6 | issue=8 | date=2011 | issn=1930-2975 | doi=10.1017/S1930297500004204 | doi-access=free | pages=771–781}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Au |first1=W. T. |title=Contemporary Psychological Research on Social Dilemmas |last2=Kwong |first2=J. Y. Y. |date=2004-06-21 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=0-521-80892-8 |editor-last=Suleiman |editor-first=Ramzi |publication-place=Cambridge, UK; New York |pages=71–98 |chapter=Measurements and Effects of Social-Value Orientation in Social Dilemmas: A Review }}</ref> [[HEXACO|Hexaco-PI-R]]<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Lee | first1=Kibeom | last2=Ashton | first2=Michael C. | title=Psychometric Properties of the HEXACO Personality Inventory | journal=Multivariate Behavioral Research | publisher=Informa UK Limited | volume=39 | issue=2 | year=2004 | issn=0027-3171 | doi=10.1207/s15327906mbr3902_8 | pages=329–358 |pmid=26804579}}</ref> and [[NEO-PI|NEO-PI-R]],<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Soto | first1=Christopher J. | last2=John | first2=Oliver P. | title=The next Big Five Inventory (BFI-2): Developing and assessing a hierarchical model with 15 facets to enhance bandwidth, fidelity, and predictive power. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | publisher=American Psychological Association (APA) | volume=113 | issue=1 | year=2017 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/pspp0000096 | doi-access=free | pages=117–143 | url=https://escholarship.org/content/qt16x6n05t/qt16x6n05t.pdf?t=qwp60t | pmid=27055049}}</ref> which also include a bit of conflict behaviour.

=== Interpersonal ===
Often, further phenomena occur in the course of a conflict. Doubts and uncertainties about one's own position are usually replaced with firm convictions ([[confirmation bias]]), without anything having changed in the real probabilities.<ref name="Ross-2013">{{Cite book |last1=Ross |first1=L. |title=Values and Knowledge |last2=Ward |first2=A. |date=2013-06-17 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-0-203-77377-2 |editor-last=Reed |editor-first=Edward S. |pages=103–135 |chapter=Naïve Realism in Everyday Life: Implications for Social Conflict and Misunderstanding |doi=10.4324/9780203773772-6 |doi-broken-date=15 July 2025 |editor-last2=Turiel |editor-first2=Elliot |editor-last3=Brown |editor-first3=Terrance}}</ref><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> Moreover, people often stick to their conviction in order not to lose face, even if the conviction is now being questioned by themselves.<ref>David C. Wilson: ''A Strategy of Change: Concepts and Controversies in the Management of Change''. ITBP Textbooks Series, Routledge series in analytical management. Thomson, 1992, ISBN 1-86152-383-1.</ref> The [[reciprocity (social psychology)|reciprocity]] ("an eye for an eye") favors a conflict escalation<ref>{{cite journal | last=Youngs | first=George A. | title=Patterns of threat and punishment reciprocity in a conflict setting. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=51 | issue=3 | date=1986 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.51.3.541 | pages=541–546 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.51.3.541 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> and a convergence of behavior when the other side consistently shows competitive or consistently collaborative behavior to achieve their goals. However, collaborative behavior tips more easily into competitive behavior than vice versa.<ref name="Forsyth2019" /> In conflicts, destructive behaviours can also appear: [[violence]], [[coercion]], [[intimidation]], [[blackmailing]], [[deception]] und [[seduction]].<ref name="Deutsch2006" />

Negative [[emotion]]s such as [[anger]] and [[fear]] make it difficult to work through the differences.<ref name=dreu/><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> Moreover, anger is often contagious, because a person who is met with anger reacts in turn more often angrily.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Van Kleef | first1=Gerben A. | last2=De Dreu | first2=Carsten K. W. | last3=Manstead | first3=Antony S. R. | title=The Interpersonal Effects of Emotions in Negotiations: A Motivated Information Processing Approach. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=87 | issue=4 | date=2004 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.87.4.510 | doi-access=free | pages=510–528 | pmid=15491275 | url=https://pure.uva.nl/ws/files/2026765/30503_Van_Kleef_de_Dreu_Manstead_2004.pdf }}</ref> Likewise, behaviors such as hostile, overly aggressive, choleric, conflict-avoiding, evasive, passive-aggressive, nagging or accusing (without changing anything), non-changing, annoying, pessimistic, superior or indecisive behavior can make conflict resolution difficult.<ref name="Deutsch2006" />

The [[attribution (psychology)|attribution]] of presumed strengths, attitudes, and values to the other conflict party is often distorted during a conflict.<ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Deutsch | editor-first=Morton | editor-last2=Coleman | editor-first2=Peter T. | editor-last3=Marcus | editor-first3=Eric C. | title=The Handbook of Conflict Resolution: Theory and Practice | publisher=John Wiley & Sons | date=2006-09-18 | isbn=978-0-7879-8666-7 |first1=L. |last1=Thompson |first2=J. |last2=Nadler |chapter=Judgmental biases in conflict resolution and how to overcome them |pages=213–235}}</ref><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> Likewise, there is often a false attribution of whether the problem arises from the situation or the character of the participants.<ref>{{cite book | last=Ross | first=Lee | title=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology | chapter=The Intuitive Psychologist And His Shortcomings: Distortions in the Attribution Process | publisher=Elsevier | volume=10 | date=1977 | isbn=978-0-12-015210-0 | doi=10.1016/s0065-2601(08)60357-3 | url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0065260108603573 | pages=173–220}}</ref> As a conflict escalates, the tactics used by each side become more confrontational (harder). However, there are situations where the threatened party fares better when a threat is not met with a counter-threat.<ref name="Borah-1963">{{cite journal | last=Borah | first=Lee A. | title=The effects of threat in bargaining: Critical and experimental analysis. | journal=The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology | volume=66 | issue=1 | date=1963 | issn=0096-851X | doi=10.1037/h0043937 | pages=37–44 | pmid=14013852 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/h0043937 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Gallo-1966">{{cite journal | last=Gallo | first=Philip S. | title=Effects of increased incentives upon the use of threat in bargaining. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=4 | issue=1 | date=1966 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/h0023510 | pages=14–20 | pmid=5965187 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/h0023510 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> A too dominant negotiation style can provoke a blocking stance as a reaction.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Curhan | first1=Jared R | last2=Neale | first2=Margaret A | last3=Ross | first3=Lee | title=Dynamic valuation: Preference changes in the context of face-to-face negotiation | journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume=40 | issue=2 | date=2004 | doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2003.12.002 | doi-access=free | pages=142–151 | url=https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/1721.1/1601/1/Dynamic%20Valuation%204253-02.pdf }}</ref><ref name="Brehm-1981">{{Cite book |last1=Brehm |first1=Sharon S. |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/C20130104230 |title=Psychological Reactance: A Theory of Freedom and Control |last2=Brehm |first2=Jack W. |date=1981 |publisher=Elsevier |isbn=978-0-12-129840-1 |language=en |doi=10.1016/C2013-0-10423-0}}</ref><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> In conflicts between equally strong parties, a competitive conflict style is avoided if a strong counter-reaction is expected.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Lawler | first1=Edward J. | last2=Ford | first2=Rebecca S. | last3=Blegen | first3=Mary A. | title=Coercive Capability in Conflict: A Test of Bilateral Deterrence Versus Conflict Spiral Theory | journal=Social Psychology Quarterly | volume=51 | issue=2 | date=1988 | doi=10.2307/2786833 | page=93 | jstor=2786833 }}</ref> While at the beginning of a conflict escalation a counter-reaction tends to be disproportionately high, it is rather disproportionately low at a higher level of escalation. Often at the beginning of a conflict escalation, various coalitions are formed to support one's own interests, which later in the conflict lead to conflicts between two groups.<ref name="Forsyth2019" />

=== Intergroup ===
When a conflict occurs not just between two individuals (interpersonal conflict), but between two or more groups (intergroup conflict), additional effects of [[group dynamics]] come into play.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Saguy | first1=Tamar | last2=Reifen-Tagar | first2=Michal | title=The social psychological roots of violent intergroup conflict | journal=Nature Reviews Psychology | volume=1 | issue=10 | date=2022-08-02 | issn=2731-0574 | doi=10.1038/s44159-022-00083-7 | pages=577–589 | url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s44159-022-00083-7 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Forsyth2019" /> Five typical emotions have been identified in groups that contribute to escalation: [[Superior (hierarchy)|superiority]], [[injustice]], [[vulnerability]], [[mistrust]], and helplessness.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Eidelson | first1=Roy J. | last2=Eidelson | first2=Judy I. | title=Dangerous ideas. Five beliefs that propel groups toward conflict | journal=The American Psychologist | volume=58 | issue=3 | date=2003 | issn=0003-066X | pmid=12772423 | doi=10.1037/0003-066x.58.3.182 | pages=182–192 }}</ref> Additionally, [[envy]], [[contempt]], [[pity]] and [[admiration]] can also occur between groups. Envy results when the out-group is perceived to have high competence, but low warmth.<ref name="Cuddy">{{cite journal | last1=Cuddy | first1=Amy J. C. | last2=Fiske | first2=Susan T. | last3=Glick | first3=Peter | title=The BIAS map: Behaviors from intergroup affect and stereotypes. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=92 | issue=4 | date=2007 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.92.4.631 | pages=631–648 | pmid=17469949 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.92.4.631 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Fiske">{{cite journal | last1=Fiske | first1=Susan T. | last2=Cuddy | first2=Amy J.C. | last3=Glick | first3=Peter | title=Universal dimensions of social cognition: warmth and competence | journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume=11 | issue=2 | date=2007 | doi=10.1016/j.tics.2006.11.005 | pages=77–83 | pmid=17188552 | url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1364661306003299 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> Envious groups are usually jealous of another group's symbolic and tangible achievements and view that group as competition. Contempt results when the out-group is taken to be low in both competence and warmth.<ref name="Cuddy" /><ref name="Fiske" /> According to Forsyth, contempt is one of the most frequent intergroup emotions. In this situation, the out-group is held responsible for its own failures. In-group members also believe that their conflict with the out-group can never be resolved. Out-groups that are believed by the in-group to be high in warmth but low in competence are pitied. Usually pitied groups are lower in status than the in-group and are not believed to be responsible for their failures.
Admiration occurs when an out-group is taken to be high in both warmth and competence, however, admiration is very rare because these two conditions are seldom met. An admired out-group is thought to be completely deserving of its accomplishments. Admiration is thought to be most likely to arise when a member of the in-group can take pride in the accomplishments of the out-group, and when the out-group achieving does not interfere with the in-group.<ref name="Forsyth2019" /><ref name="Cuddy" /><ref name="Fiske" />

Groups often exhibit more competitive behavior than individuals within a group do with each other. Merely perceiving one's own [[group identity]] already favors discrimination against foreign groups. When individuals with a collaborative conflict style join a group, a switch to a competitive group conflict style ([[social group|group behavior]]) can occur. Additionally, other effects of [[social dominance theory|dominant behavior]] within the group and between groups come into play. Motivations such as [[desire|greed]], fear, and [[social identity theory|social identity]] increase in groups. If the potential reward for greed is reduced, the effect of greed diminishes. There is a [[double standard]] that manifests itself primarily in an enhancement of the actions of one's own group, but also in a devaluation of the actions of other groups. This also includes distorted [[generalization]]s and [[stereotype]]s attributed to the other group. It involves both [[individuation|deindividuation]] (opponents are only perceived as part of a homogeneous group, not as individuals), and [[dehumanization]] (opponents are perceived as subhuman).<ref name="Deutsch2006" /> In an experiment, more than half of the participants opted for a choice with less reward if the process was perceived as [[Procedural justice|fair]] in return.<ref name="Forsyth2019" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sidanius |first1=Jim |last2=Haley |first2=Hillary |last3=Molina |first3=Ludwin |last4=Pratto |first4=Felicia |date=2007 |title=Vladimir's Choice and the Distribution of Social Resources: A Group Dominance Perspective |url=https://hal.science/hal-00571648/document |journal=Group Processes & Intergroup Relations |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=257–265 |doi=10.1177/1368430207074732 |hdl=2262/52378 |issn=1368-4302 |doi-access=free|hdl-access=free }}</ref>

A key player in inter-group relations and conflict is the [[collective]] sentiment a person's own group (in-group) feels toward another group (out-group). These inter-group emotions are usually negative, and range in intensity from feelings of discomfort when interacting with a member of a certain other group to full on hatred for another group and its members. For example, in Fischer's organizational research at the [[University of Oxford]], inter-group conflict was so 'heated' that it became mutually destructive and intractable, resulting in organizational collapse.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Fischer|first=Michael D.|date=September 2012|title=Organizational Turbulence, Trouble and Trauma: Theorizing the Collapse of a Mental Health Setting|journal=Organization Studies|language=en|volume=33|issue=9|pages=1153–1173|doi=10.1177/0170840612448155|s2cid=52219788|issn=0170-8406}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Fischer|first1=Michael Daniel|last2=Ferlie|first2=Ewan|date=January 2013|title=Resisting hybridisation between modes of clinical risk management: Contradiction, contest, and the production of intractable conflict|journal=Accounting, Organizations and Society|language=en|volume=38|issue=1|pages=30–49|doi=10.1016/j.aos.2012.11.002|s2cid=44146410|url=https://researchbank.acu.edu.au/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1701&context=flb_pub }}</ref>

Out-group-directed emotions can be expressed both verbally and non-verbally, and according to the [[stereotype]] content model, are dictated by two dimensions: the perceived warmth (friendliness) and competence of the other group (skillfulness). Depending on the perceived degree of warmth and competence, the stereotype content model predicts four basic emotions that could be directed toward the out-group (Forsyth, 2010).

== Factors ==
Although the involved parties may hope to reach a solution to their dispute quickly, psychological and interpersonal factors can frustrate their attempts to control the conflict, and in this case, [[conflict escalation]] occurs. Conflict escalation "can be understood as an intensification of a conflict with regard to the observed extent and the means used".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bösch|first=Richard|title=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of International Studies |date=2017-11-20|chapter=Conflict Escalation|chapter-url=https://oxfordre.com/internationalstudies/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780190846626.001.0001/acrefore-9780190846626-e-82|access-date=2020-07-18|publisher=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of International Studies|language=en|doi=10.1093/acrefore/9780190846626.013.82|isbn=978-0-19-084662-6}}</ref> A number of factors including increased commitment to one's position, use of harder influence tactics, and formation of coalitions propel the escalation of the conflict.<ref>{{cite book |last=Forsyth |first=D.R. |year=2010 |title=Group Dynamics |edition=5th |location=Belmont, CA |publisher=Wadsworth}}</ref>

=== Uncertainty and commitment ===
As conflicts escalate, group members' doubts and uncertainties are replaced with a firm commitment to their position. People rationalize their choices once they have made them: they seek out information that supports their views, reject information that disconfirms their views, and become more entrenched in their original position (also see [[Confirmation bias|confirmatory bias]]).<ref name="Ross-2013"/> Additionally, people believe that once they commit to a position publicly, they should stick with it. Sometimes, they may realize the shortcomings of their views, but they continue defending those views and arguing against their opponents just to save face.<ref>{{cite book |last=Wilson |first=D. C. |year=1992 |title=A Strategy of Change |location=London |publisher=Routledge}}</ref> Finally, if the opponents argue too strongly, reactance may set in and group members become even more committed to the position.<ref name="Brehm-1981"/><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Curhan | first1=Jared R | last2=Neale | first2=Margaret A | last3=Ross | first3=Lee | title=Dynamic valuation: Preference changes in the context of face-to-face negotiation | journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | publisher=Elsevier BV | volume=40 | issue=2 | year=2004 | issn=0022-1031 | doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2003.12.002 | doi-access=free | pages=142–151 | url=https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/1721.1/1601/1/Dynamic%20Valuation%204253-02.pdf }}</ref>

=== Perception and misperception ===
Individuals' reactions to the conflict are shaped by their perception of the situation and people in the situation. During the conflict, opponents' inferences about each other's strengths, attitudes, values, and personal qualities tend to be largely distorted.<ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Deutsch | editor-first=Morton | editor-last2=Coleman | editor-first2=Peter T. | editor-last3=Marcus | editor-first3=Eric C. | title=The Handbook of Conflict Resolution: Theory and Practice | publisher=John Wiley & Sons | date=2006-09-18 | isbn=978-0-7879-8666-7 |last1=Thompson |first1=L. |last2=Nadler |first2=J. |chapter=Judgmental biases in conflict resolution and how to overcome them |pages= 213–235}}</ref>

=== Misattribution ===
During the conflict, people explain their opponents' actions in ways that make the problem worse. [[Fundamental attribution error]] occurs when one assumes that opponents' behavior was caused by personal (dispositional) rather than situational (environmental) factors.<ref>{{cite book | last=Ross | first=Lee | title=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology | chapter=The Intuitive Psychologist And His Shortcomings: Distortions in the Attribution Process | publisher=Elsevier | year=1977 | volume=10 | isbn=978-0-12-015210-0 | issn=0065-2601 | doi=10.1016/s0065-2601(08)60357-3 | pages=173–220}}</ref> When conflict continues for a while, opponents might decide that this conflict is intractable. People usually expect intractable conflicts to be prolonged, intense, and very hard to resolve.<ref>{{cite book |last=Bar-Tal |first=D. |year=2007 |title=Living with the conflict: Socio-psychological analysis of the Israeli-Jewish society |location=Jerusalem |publisher=Carmel |language= Hebrew}}</ref>

=== Misperceiving motivations ===
During the conflict, opponents often become mistrustful of one another wondering if their cooperative motivations were replaced by competitive ones. This loss of trust makes it difficult to return to the cooperative relationship. People with competitive social value orientations ([[Social value orientations|SVOs]]) are the most inaccurate in their perception of opponents' motivation. They often think that others compete with them when in fact, there is no competition going on.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sattler |first1=D. N. |last2=Kerr |first2=N. L. |year=1991 |title=Might versus morality explored: Motivational and cognitive bases for social motives |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=60 |issue=5 |pages=756–765 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.60.5.756 }}</ref> Competitors are also more biased in their search for information that confirms their suspicions that others compete with them.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Kleef | first1=Gerben A. Van | last2=Dreu | first2=Carsten K.W. De | title=Social value orientation and impression formation: A test of two competing hypotheses about information search in negotiation | journal=International Journal of Conflict Management | publisher=MCB UP Ltd | volume=13 | issue=1 | date=2002-01-01 | pages=59–77 | doi=10.1108/eb022867 | url=https://www.emerald.com/insight/content/doi/10.1108/eb022867/full/html | url-access=subscription }}</ref> They also tend to deliberately misrepresent their intentions, sometimes claiming to be more cooperatively oriented than they actually are.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Steinel | first1=Wolfgang | last2=De Dreu | first2=Carsten K. W. | title=Social Motives and Strategic Misrepresentation in Social Decision Making. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=86 | issue=3 | date=2004 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.3.419 | doi-access=free | pages=419–434 | pmid=15008646 | hdl=1887/14958 | url=https://scholarlypublications.universiteitleiden.nl/access/item%3A2863751/view | hdl-access=free }}</ref>

=== Soft tactics and hard tactics ===
People use soft tactics at the outset of the conflict, but as it escalates, tactics become stronger and harder. To demonstrate this phenomenon, Mikolic, Parker, and Pruitt (1997)<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Mikolic | first1=Joseph M. | last2=Parker | first2=John C. | last3=Pruitt | first3=Dean G. | title=Escalation in response to persistent annoyance: Groups versus individuals and gender effects. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=72 | issue=1 | date=1997 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.72.1.151 | pages=151–163 | pmid=9008378 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.72.1.151 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> simulated a conflict situation by creating a "[[birthday card]] factory" with study participants who were paid a small amount for each card they manufactured using paper, colored markers, and ribbons. The work went well until researchers' confederate who posed as another participant started hoarding production materials. Initially, group members tried to solve the problem with statements and requests. When these methods failed they shifted to demands and complaints, and then to threats, abuse, and anger.

Although hard tactics can overwhelm the opponent, they often intensify conflicts. Morton Deutsch and Robert Krauss (1960)<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Deutsch | first1=Morton | last2=Krauss | first2=Robert M. | title=The effect of threat upon interpersonal bargaining. | journal=The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology | volume=61 | issue=2 | date=1960 | issn=0096-851X | doi=10.1037/h0042589 | pages=181–189 | pmid=13722331 | url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/h0042589 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> used trucking game experiment to demonstrate that capacity to threaten others intensifies conflict. They also showed that establishing a communication link does not always help to solve the dispute.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Krauss |first1=R. M.  |last2=Morsella |first2=E. |chapter=Communication and conflict. | editor-last=Deutsch | editor-first=Morton | editor-last2=Coleman | editor-first2=Peter T. | editor-last3=Marcus | editor-first3=Eric C. | title=The Handbook of Conflict Resolution: Theory and Practice | publisher=John Wiley & Sons | date=2011-03-04 | isbn=978-1-118-04690-6 |pages=144–157}}</ref> If one party threatens the other, the threatened party will sometimes fare best if it cannot respond with a counterthreat.<ref name="Borah-1963"/><ref name="Gallo-1966"/> Equally powerful opponents, however, learn to avoid the use of power if the fear of retaliation is high.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Lawler | first1=Edward J. | last2=Ford | first2=Rebecca S. | last3=Blegen | first3=Mary A. | title=Coercive Capability in Conflict: A Test of Bilateral Deterrence Versus Conflict Spiral Theory | journal=Social Psychology Quarterly | publisher=[Sage Publications, Inc., American Sociological Association] | volume=51 | issue=2 | year=1988 | issn=0190-2725 | jstor=2786833 | pages=93–107 | doi=10.2307/2786833 }}</ref>

=== Reciprocity and upward conflict spiral ===
In many cases, upward conflict spirals are sustained by the norms of reciprocity: if one group or person criticizes the other, the criticized person or group feels justified in doing the same. In conflict situations, opponents often follow the norm of rough reciprocity, i.e. they give too much (overmatching) or too little (undermatching) in return. At low levels of conflict, opponents overmatch their threats, while at high levels of conflict they undermatch their threats. Overmatching may serve as a strong warning, while undermatching may be used to send conciliatory messages.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Youngs |first=G. A., Jr. |year=1986 |title=Patterns of threat and punishment reciprocity in a conflict setting |journal=Journal of Personality & Social Psychology |volume=51 |issue=3 |pages=541–546 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.51.3.541 }}</ref>

=== Few and many ===
When conflicts erupt, group members use coalitions to shift the balance of power in their favor, and it is typical for multiparty conflicts to reduce to two-party blocks over time. Coalitions contribute to the conflict because they draw more members of the group into the affray.  Individuals in coalitions work not only to ensure their own outcomes but also to worsen outcomes of non-coalition members. Those who are excluded from the coalition react with hostility and try to regain power by forming their own coalition. Thus, coalitions need to be constantly maintained through strategic bargaining and negotiation.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Mannix | first=Elizabeth A. | title=Organizations as Resource Dilemmas: The Effects of Power Balance on Coalition Formation in Small Groups | journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes | publisher=Elsevier BV | volume=55 | issue=1 | year=1993 | issn=0749-5978 | doi=10.1006/obhd.1993.1021 | pages=1–22}}</ref>

=== Irritation and anger ===
It is generally difficult for most people to remain calm and collected in a conflict situation. However, an increase in [[negative emotion]]s (i.e. anger) only exacerbates the initial conflict. Even when group members want to discuss their positions calmly and dispassionately, once they become committed to their positions, an emotional expression often replaces logical discussion.<ref name=dreu>{{cite book| last1=De Dreu | first1=C.K.W. | last2=Beersma | first2=B. | last3=Steinel | first3=W. | last4=Kleef | first4=G.A. van | chapter=The psychology of negotiation: Principles and basic processes. | publisher=Guilford | date=2007 |editor1=A. W. Kruglanski |editor2= E. T. Higgins |title=Social Psychology: Handbook of Basic Principles |edition=2nd |pages=608–629 |location=New York}}</ref> Anger is also contagious: when group member negotiates with someone who is angry, they become angry themselves.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Van Kleef | first1=Gerben A. | last2=De Dreu | first2=Carsten K. W. | last3=Manstead | first3=Antony S. R. | title=The Interpersonal Effects of Emotions in Negotiations: A Motivated Information Processing Approach. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=87 | issue=4 | date=2004 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.87.4.510 | doi-access=free | pages=510–528 | url=https://pure.uva.nl/ws/files/2026765/30503_Van_Kleef_de_Dreu_Manstead_2004.pdf | pmid=15491275}}</ref>

==Styles==

Thomas and Kilmann distinguish five typical conflict styles:<ref>{{cite web |website=[[University of Cambridge]] Judge Business School |url=https://www.jbs.cam.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/understanding-conflict.pdf |title=Understanding conflict: The Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument |access-date= February 11, 2024}}</ref>

{| class=wikitable
|- id="Conflict situation" 
! Conflict style !! Pros and Cons !! Situations 
|- 
| Competitive<br />(win-lose)|| {{ulist| Pursuit of own objectives| Use of power| Can lead to disputes| Can cause resentments}} || {{ulist| Emergencies requiring quick decisions| Important and unpopular decisions| When you are certain you are right (important matters)| To defend against others taking advantage }}
|-
| Collaborative<br />([[win-win]])|| {{ulist| Cooperation to everyone's satisfaction| Detailed analysis of interests| Involves cognitive conflict| Can be time-consuming}} || {{ulist| When a compromise is not acceptable| To gain support for the cause| To maintain or improve relationships| To unify perspectives }}
|- 
| Compromise-seeking<br />(½win-½win)|| {{ulist| Middle ground regarding assertiveness and cooperativity| Splitting the difference| Neither half nor whole?}} || {{ulist| Temporary solutions for complex conflicts| When goals of two equally strong opponents are mutually exclusive| Under time pressure| Evasive strategy for collaborative or competitive }}
|- 
| Avoiding<br />(lose-lose)|| {{ulist| Neglect of both interests| Conflicts remain unresolved| Delaying tactic}} || {{ulist| Hopeless situations outside of one's control| Unimportant situations| To let others cool down| When others can resolve the conflict more effectively }}
|- 
| Accommodating<br />(lose-win) || {{ulist| Opposite of competitive| Self-sacrifice| Selfless generosity| Doormat}} || {{ulist| When the other person's issues are much more important| To build social capital| When clearly inferior and losing| When harmony is especially important| To allow subordinates to develop }}
|- 
|}

Limited resources can be distributed either according to the previous [[investment]] of time, energy and resources, according to [[egalitarianism|equal shares]], according to [[power (social and political)|power]] ratios or according to [[need]].<ref name="Forsyth2019" /> A decision can be reached using various procedures. Ideally, a [[consensus decision-making|consensus]] is worked on collaboratively (and preferably on a win-win solution) because this serves the interests of all parties involved. In situations where the interests are immovable and mutually exclusive, other procedures must be used. Common procedures in which only some of the interests of both sides are served are [[compromise]] procedures or [[distributive justice|distribution]] imposed by a neutral authority (as in the inquisitorial or arbitration procedure, with parents or a supervisor). Furthermore, a [[vote]] or a judicial [[judgment]] is often carried out as a competitive procedure, in which the interests of the larger group or the right-preserving side are served first. Likewise, a [[concession (politics)|concession]] by one side can resolve the conflict if there is a willingness to do so. As a concession implies at least a partial renunciation of one's own interests with little to no compensation, a willingness to do so becomes less likely with increasing escalation.

According to Ramsbotham, conflicts are divided into five phases of conflict development, to which three conflict resolution strategies are assigned:<ref name="Bösch" />
* Phase of differences: conflict transformation
* Phase of objection: conflict transformation
* Phase of polarization: settlement of conflicts
* Phase of violence: settlement of conflicts
* Phase of war: containment of conflicts

While the Thomas and Kilmann system describes five typical conflict styles, aligns the measures with the conflict styles and the situations and emphasizes collaboration as a solution, the measures in the Glasl system and the Ramsbotham system are aligned with the escalation level.

==Resolution==
{{excerpt| Conflict resolution| paragraphs=1,2}}

==Mediation==
{{main|Mediation}}
Conflict is a social process that is exacerbated when individual members of a group take sides in the debate. Among the methods to resolve conflict is [[mediation]] of the dispute by a group member not currently involved in the dispute. More specifically, a mediator is defined as a person who attempts to resolve a conflict between two group members by intervening in this conflict. Put simply, the mediator can be thought of as a disinterested guide directs the disputants through the process of developing a solution to a disagreement.<ref name="Forsyth2019" /> For conflicts with negative [[interpersonal relationship]]s on a low escalation level, [[Team building|relationship building]] can help transform the nature of the relationship and improve the communication.<ref name="Deutsch2006" /> As mediation depends on meeting together peacefully, it is more successful in conflicts with low levels of escalation where there is still a will to work on an agreement.

Although the tendency will be for group members who are uninvolved in the dispute to remain uninvolved,  in some cases, the sheer intensity of the conflict may escalate to the point where mediation is unavoidable and still feasible. Third party mediation of the conflict opens avenues for communication between group members in conflict. It allows members to express their opinions and request clarification of other member's standpoints while the mediator acts as a form of protection against any shame or "loss of face" that either disputant may experience. This can be done by shedding a positive light on the reconciliation that was made during the mediation process. For instance, if it was negotiated that two cashiers will rotate the weekends they work, the mediator might point out that now each worker gets a weekend off every two weeks.<ref name="Forsyth2010">{{harvnb|Forsyth|2010}}</ref> The mediator can also offer assistance in refining solutions and making counter-offers between members, adjusting the time and location of meetings so that they are mutually satisfying for both parties.<ref name="Forsyth2010" />
There are three major mediation approaches:

# Inquisitorial procedure: Using this procedure, the mediator asks each of the disputants a series of questions, considers the two sets of responses, and then selects and imposes a mandatory solution on the members. The inquisitorial procedure is the least popular approach to mediation.
# Arbitration: Here, mediation involves the two disputants explaining their arguments to the mediator, who creates a solution based on the arguments presented. Arbitration is best for low intensity conflict, but is the most favored mediation style overall.
# Moot: The moot approach involves an open discussion  between disputants and the mediator about the problems and potential solutions. In the moot approach, the mediator cannot impose a mandatory solution. After arbitration, a moot is the most preferred mediation style. It does not always lead to a tangible result.

In practice, conflict resolution is often interwoven with daily activities, as in organizations, workplaces and institutions. Staff and residents in a youth care setting, for instance, interweave everyday concerns (meals, lessons, breaks, meetings, or other mundane but concerted projects) with interpersonal disputes.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wästerfors |first=David |date=2010-08-18 |title=Disputes and Going Concerns in an Institution for "Troublesome" Boys |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0891241610377199 |journal=Journal of Contemporary Ethnography |volume=40 |pages=39–70 |publisher=SAGE PublicationsSage CA: Los Angeles, CA |doi=10.1177/0891241610377199 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Institutionalization==
[[File:KUNA 10 (05) (cropped).jpg|thumb|Military forces, such as the [[Polish Armed Forces]] seen here, are an example of an institution that handles conflict.]]
The institutionalization of conflicts refers to the resolution or settlement of a conflict when it has been passed on to an [[institution]].<ref>{{cite book |author=[[Ralf Dahrendorf]] |title=Pfade aus Utopia |location=München |year=1974 |isbn=978-3-4921-0101-1 |language=de | publisher=Piper}}</ref> However, delegation to institutions brings about a lack of [[freedom]] in terms of the distribution structure of [[Entitlement (fair division)|entitlement]]s and offers. The parties to the conflict are directed by persons not involved in the conflict. In this process, the emotional and factual components of the conflict are separated. The institution or instance proceeds with rules that are mutually recognized by the parties to the conflict.

=== Feud ===
Conflicts can be strongly or weakly institutionalized. The [[feud]], a weakly institutionalized conflict, has some rules that are recognized by the parties to the conflict (e.g., existence of a legitimate feud reason, formal announcement, procedure, etc.), all signs of institutionalization, but on the other hand, the conflict is handled by the parties to the conflict themselves (no social differentiation); the emotional and factual component of the conflict are not separated: friends of the respective parties to the conflict not only have the right, but even the duty to assist, and they generate further conflicts.

===Justice system===
{{Main|Justice system}}
The justice system is a [[nation]]al competitive system (interest of one party is to be served) for the regulation of conflicts. The procedures are divided into [[criminal procedure]]s and [[civil procedure]]s. The procedures are used when a [[legal claim]] is to be negotiated.

=== Military ===
{{Main|Military}}
The military is used, among other things, in the event of a very strong escalation of a conflict between states or paramilitary groups. It is a competitive system with comparatively strong damages and collateral damages and is therefore used as a last resort. Early written works on military conflict resolution are ''[[The Art of War]]'' by [[Sunzi]]<ref>{{cite book |translator=[[Lionel Giles]] |url=https://archive.org/details/Suntzu-ArtOfWar |title=The Art of War.|date=1910 |via= [[Internet Archive]]}}</ref> and ''[[On War]]'' by [[Carl von Clausewitz]].<ref>{{cite book |author=[[Carl von Clausewitz]] |title=Vom Kriege |volume=1–3 |publisher=Ferdinand Dümmler |location=Berlin |date=1832–1834 |editor=[[Marie von Clausewitz]] |url=http://www.clausewitz.com/readings/VomKriege1832/TOC.htm#TOC}}</ref>

===Divorce===
{{Main|Divorce}}
A divorce is a judicial conflict management system. If there are children, sometimes mediation, [[counseling]] or [[child protective services]] are used following a separation of parents.

===Company conflicts===
{{Main|Organizational conflict}}
Conflicts between employees of a [[company]] can create [[conflict cost]] to the company.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Gaba | first1=Vibha | last2=Joseph | first2=John | title=Content and process: organizational conflict and decision making | journal=Frontiers in Psychology | publisher=Frontiers Media SA | volume=14 | date=2023-11-14 | article-number=1227966 | issn=1664-1078 | doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1227966 | doi-access=free |pmid= 38034290 |pmc=10681961}}</ref> There is [[On-the-job training]] and [[Coaching]] for conflict management.
